skill.name: conductor-implement
skill.description: Guide Conductor track implementation with TDD, progress tracking, verification checkpoints, and commit workflow steps.
skill.summary: Turns Conductor implementation plans into verified task-by-task delivery steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk matches, but the weak cryptography and network reconnaissance hits are false positives from Markdown text, file extensions, and placeholders. The real confirmed risk is legitimate workflow guidance that asks the agent to edit repository files, run test commands, and create git commits. No prompt injection attempt, data exfiltration pattern, or malicious network behavior was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Executes Repository Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run project tests and git commands during implementation. This is expected for a coding workflow, but it can execute repository code and should be used only in trusted workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Markdown explicitly directs test execution and git add or commit operations. The intent is legitimate delivery automation, but the command execution risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill directs updates to tracking documents, metadata files, test files, implementation code, and completion records. These edits are core to the workflow but can affect many repository files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write behavior is documented directly in the workflow. It is not malicious, but users should expect repository modifications.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Pattern Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and network reconnaissance alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code or network probing. They appear to be triggered by Markdown text, file names, placeholders, and command examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic algorithm use and no network reconnaissance instructions at these lines. The cited content is workflow prose or placeholders.

low_findings.title: Broad Git Staging Example
low_findings.description: The workflow shows git add -A before committing task changes. This can stage unrelated local changes if the agent does not inspect the working tree first.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present in the Markdown example. It is common in workflows, but it has a clear operational risk in dirty repositories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples for git and test execution. These commands are not hidden or obfuscated, but they increase risk when applied to untrusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are visible in fenced code or inline command text. Their purpose is legitimate implementation workflow control.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository State File Updates
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow updates plan, metadata, and status files while implementing track tasks. This is expected behavior but should be reviewed before publishing changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly require these file updates. The risk is bounded to repository workflow files and implementation outputs.

content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable way to move Conductor plans into verified code. This skill guides track selection, TDD execution, progress updates, commits, and phase checkpoints.
actual_capabilities: Checks that required Conductor project files exist before implementation starts.
Loads workflow rules, track plans, metadata, product context, and technical constraints.
Guides task execution through TDD red, green, and refactor phases when configured.
Updates task status, track metadata, and completion records during implementation.
Runs verification checkpoints and asks for approval before moving between phases.
Provides failure handling options for tool errors, test failures, and git problems.
limitations: Requires an existing Conductor directory with product, workflow, tracks, and plan files.
Relies on the project test commands and repository conventions already in place.
May create git commits when the configured workflow requests task-level commits.
Does not replace human approval for failed tests, phase gates, or risky changes.

use_cases.title: Implement a planned feature track
use_cases.description: Turn a Conductor feature plan into task-by-task code changes with test execution and status updates.

use_cases.title: Resume paused implementation work
use_cases.description: Load current metadata, find the active task, and continue from the recorded implementation state.

use_cases.title: Enforce phase verification gates
use_cases.description: Run phase checks, summarize results, and wait for approval before moving to the next phase.

prompt_templates.title: Start the next track
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for selecting a pending track.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use conductor-implement to find the next pending Conductor track, load its plan, and start the first incomplete task.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a specific task
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused prompt for a known task identifier.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use conductor-implement for track auth_20250115 and implement Task 2.3. Follow the configured TDD workflow and update progress files.

prompt_templates.title: Resume paused work
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for continuing a track from metadata.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use conductor-implement to resume the current in-progress track. Read metadata, confirm the active task, and present resume options before editing.

prompt_templates.title: Complete phase with verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use conductor-implement to finish the current phase, run all required verification, summarize results, and wait for approval before continuing.

output_examples.input: Start implementation for a pending navigation fix track.
output: Loaded Conductor workflow and selected the navigation fix track.
Marked the first incomplete task as in progress.
Created or updated tests, ran verification, and reported the result.
Asked for approval before moving past the phase checkpoint.

output_examples.input: Resume an authentication track after a failed test run.
output: Read the saved metadata and found the active task.
Displayed the failing test summary and available recovery options.
Paused for user choice before retrying, rolling back, or stopping work.

output_examples.input: Finish all tasks in a dashboard track.
output: Updated task, phase, and track status records.
Ran final verification against acceptance criteria.
Offered documentation sync and cleanup choices.

best_practices: Review workflow.md and the selected plan before allowing file edits or commits.
Run the skill in a clean git worktree so generated commits contain only intended changes.
Require explicit human approval at phase checkpoints and after any failed command.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill outside a repository initialized with Conductor files.
Do not skip failing tests or verification checkpoints to finish a track faster.
Do not allow broad git staging when unrelated local changes are present.

faq.question: What does this skill implement?
faq.answer: It implements tasks from a Conductor track plan and keeps plan, metadata, and status files updated.

faq.question: Does it require TDD?
faq.answer: It follows the TDD strictness configured in conductor/workflow.md. It can also handle non-TDD workflows when allowed.

faq.question: Can it choose a track automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. If no track is provided, it reads conductor/tracks.md and presents pending or in-progress tracks.

faq.question: Will it run commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow can run project tests and git commands, so use it only in trusted repositories.

faq.question: How does it handle failures?
faq.answer: It stops on tool, test, or git failures and presents recovery options before continuing.

faq.question: Does it finish tracks without approval?
faq.answer: No. It requires explicit user approval before moving between verified phases.
